paper_info.author: WJ Chng, N Gonzalez-Paz, T Price-Troska, S Jacobus, SV Rajkumar, MM Oken, RA Kyle, KJ Henderson, S Van Wier, P Greipp, B Van Ness, R Fonseca

evidence_items.evidence_description: KRAS mutations are significantly associated with shorter overall survival and progression-free survival in multiple myeloma patients. When analyzed separately, only KRAS mutations (not NRAS) showed significant association with survival outcomes. In the ECOG cohort of 439 newly diagnosed patients, KRAS mutations were detected in 28 patients (6.4%) at codons 12, 13, 61, 16, 22, and 24, with codons 12 and 13 being most common (22 of 28 mutations). KRAS mutant tumors were significantly associated with plasma cell labeling index greater than 1% (50% versus 27%, P=0.023), indicating higher proliferation. In Cox regression analysis for overall RAS mutations, there was a 47% increase in hazard of death (P=0.045), with KRAS mutations driving this prognostic impact.
evidence_items.verbatim_quote: Patients with RAS mutations have significantly shorter overall survival and progression free survival despite similar response to therapy (Figure 1). Interestingly, when we analyzed the impact of KRAS and NRAS mutations on survival separately, only KRAS and not NRAS mutations were significantly associated with shorter overall survival and progression free survival (Figure 1). RAS mutation was an independent prognostic factor for overall survival but not progression free survival in the Cox proportional hazards regression analyses. For overall survival, selection results included ISS stage III, PCLI greater than 1, t(4;14), and 17p13 deletion/TP53 mutation. Stage II was forced back into the model for interpretation. Finally, adding RAS mutation improved the model slightly based on likelihood-ratio χ2 P-value 0.052. Patients with RAS mutation had a 47% increase in the hazard of death compared with non-RAS patients (P-value of 0.045) after adjusting for all other variables in the model (Table 2).
evidence_items.quote_snippet: only KRAS and not NRAS mutations were significantly associated with shorter overall survival and progression free survival
evidence_items.extraction_reasoning: This is highly actionable prognostic evidence. KRAS mutations independently predict shorter survival in multiple myeloma, which could influence risk stratification and treatment intensity decisions. The evidence is from a large cohort study (N=439) with multivariate analysis. Clarified that the 47% hazard increase is from combined RAS analysis, but KRAS specifically drives the survival impact.
evidence_items.source_title: Clinical and biological significance of RAS mutations in multiple myeloma

evidence_items.evidence_description: NRAS mutations are associated with aggressive disease features in multiple myeloma including higher plasma cell percentage in bone marrow, greater percentage with B2M greater than 3.5 g/L, greater percentage with ISS stage II and III disease, lower hemoglobin levels, and more frequent lytic bone lesions. In the ECOG cohort of 439 newly diagnosed patients, 74 patients (16.9%) had NRAS mutations. The majority of NRAS mutations (64 of 74) occurred at codon 61, with 5 mutations at codons 12 and 13, and additional mutations at codons 64 and 86. NRAS mutations were more common than KRAS mutations (72% vs 28% of all RAS mutations). These mutations identify patients with higher tumor burden and more advanced disease stage, though NRAS mutations alone (when analyzed separately from KRAS) were not significantly associated with survival outcomes.
evidence_items.verbatim_quote: RAS mutation is significantly associated with higher plasma cell percentage in the marrow, greater percentage with B2M greater than 3.5 g/l, greater percentage with ISS stage II and III disease, κ light chain secretion, lower hemoglobin level and more frequent lytic bone lesions. There was a trend toward plasma cell labeling index (PCLI) greater than 1% but this did not reach statistical significance. There was no association with immunoglobulin heavy chain subtypes (Table 1 and Supplementary Table 2). Similar associations were seen when KRAS and NRAS mutations were analyzed separately (data not shown) except that tumors with KRAS mutations are significantly associated with PCLI greater than 1% (50 versus 27%, P = 0.023). Seventy-four (72%) patients had mutations in NRAS. The majority of these mutations were in codon 61 (64 of the 74), with 5 mutations detected in codons 12 and 13. We also found mutations in codon 64 (n = 1) and codon 86 (n = 4). Interestingly, when we analyzed the impact of KRAS and NRAS mutations on survival separately, only KRAS and not NRAS mutations were significantly associated with shorter overall survival and progression free survival (Figure 1).
evidence_items.quote_snippet: RAS mutation is significantly associated with higher plasma cell percentage, greater ISS stage II and III disease, lower hemoglobin level and more frequent lytic bone lesions
evidence_items.extraction_reasoning: NRAS mutations are actionable for identifying patients with aggressive disease features and higher tumor burden. While not independently associated with survival outcomes, they help characterize disease biology and could inform monitoring intensity and treatment decisions based on disease burden markers.
evidence_items.source_title: Clinical and biological significance of RAS mutations in multiple myeloma

evidence_items.evidence_description: RAS mutations (KRAS or NRAS) are independent prognostic factors for overall survival in multiple myeloma. In multivariate Cox regression analysis of 439 newly diagnosed patients, RAS mutations showed a 47% increase in the hazard of death (P=0.045) after adjusting for ISS stage III, PCLI greater than 1%, t(4;14), 17p13 deletion/TP53 mutation, and stage II disease. Adding RAS mutation status improved the prognostic model with likelihood-ratio χ2 P-value of 0.052. RAS mutations were detected in 102 patients (23%) and were associated with aggressive disease features including higher tumor burden markers. The prognostic impact remained significant even after accounting for other known risk factors in multiple myeloma.
evidence_items.verbatim_quote: RAS mutation was an independent prognostic factor for overall survival but not progression free survival in the Cox proportional hazards regression analyses. For overall survival, selection results included ISS stage III, PCLI greater than 1, t(4;14), and 17p13 deletion/TP53 mutation. Stage II was forced back into the model for interpretation. Finally, adding RAS mutation improved the model slightly based on likelihood-ratio χ2 P-value 0.052. Patients with RAS mutation had a 47% increase in the hazard of death compared with non-RAS patients (P-value of 0.045) after adjusting for all other variables in the model (Table 2).
evidence_items.quote_snippet: Patients with RAS mutation had a 47% increase in the hazard of death compared with non-RAS patients (P-value of 0.045) after adjusting for all other variables
evidence_items.extraction_reasoning: This is highly actionable prognostic evidence with independent predictive value. RAS mutation status could be used for risk stratification in newly diagnosed multiple myeloma patients to guide treatment intensity. The 47% increased hazard of death is clinically significant and independent of other established risk factors.
evidence_items.source_title: Clinical and biological significance of RAS mutations in multiple myeloma

evidence_items.evidence_description: RAS mutations are associated with malignant transformation from monoclonal gammopathy of undetermined significance (MGUS) to multiple myeloma. In the Mayo Clinic cohort, RAS mutations were detected in 1 of 14 MGUS patients (7%), 15 of 60 newly diagnosed MM patients (25%), and 10 of 22 relapsed MM patients (45%). The low incidence in MGUS compared to newly diagnosed MM (7% vs 25%) and the even higher incidence in relapsed MM (45%) demonstrates that RAS mutations are important transforming events in myeloma progression. NRAS mutations were more common than KRAS mutations across all disease stages. This finding is consistent with previous studies showing RAS mutations as a recurrent difference between MGUS and MM.
evidence_items.verbatim_quote: In the Mayo Clinic cohort, RAS mutation was detected in 1 of 14 MGUS patients (7%), 15 of 60 newly diagnosed MM patients (25%) and 10 of 22 relapsed MM patients (45%). Once again N-RAS mutations were more common that K-RAS mutations. Our study confirms the low incidence of RAS mutation in MGUS compared to MM found in previous studies, consistent with RAS mutation being an important transforming event from MGUS to MM. One of the recurrent differences between MGUS and MM is the presence of RAS mutations in the latter. RAS mutations may, therefore, play an important role in malignant transformation of clonal plasma cells and myeloma pathogenesis.
evidence_items.quote_snippet: RAS mutation was detected in 1 of 14 MGUS patients (7%), 15 of 60 newly diagnosed MM patients (25%) and 10 of 22 relapsed MM patients (45%)
evidence_items.extraction_reasoning: This is actionable diagnostic evidence showing RAS mutations as markers of disease transformation. The clear progression from 7% in MGUS to 25% in newly diagnosed MM to 45% in relapsed MM suggests RAS mutations could inform monitoring strategies for MGUS patients and help identify those at higher risk for progression.
evidence_items.source_title: Clinical and biological significance of RAS mutations in multiple myeloma

evidence_items.evidence_description: KRAS mutations are significantly associated with TP53 mutations in multiple myeloma, identifying a high-risk molecular subgroup with poor prognosis. In the ECOG cohort of 439 patients, 15% of patients with KRAS mutations also had TP53 mutations compared to only 3% of patients without KRAS mutations (P=0.014). This co-occurrence may partly explain the predominant prognostic impact of KRAS mutations on survival, as TP53 mutations are known to be powerful adverse prognostic factors in multiple myeloma. NRAS mutations did not show this association with TP53. This finding suggests KRAS mutations may identify patients who warrant testing for TP53 status and potentially more aggressive treatment approaches.
evidence_items.verbatim_quote: Interestingly, KRAS but not NRAS mutation was significantly associated with TP53 mutation (15% of those with KRAS mutation versus 3% of those without, P = 0.014). Interestingly, the predominant prognostic impact of RAS mutation is mediated through KRAS mutation. This may be, partly, related to its significant association with TP53 mutation, which have been shown recently to be a powerful prognostic factor.
evidence_items.quote_snippet: KRAS but not NRAS mutation was significantly associated with TP53 mutation (15% versus 3%, P = 0.014)
evidence_items.extraction_reasoning: This is actionable prognostic evidence for identifying a high-risk subgroup. The association between KRAS and TP53 mutations helps explain KRAS's prognostic impact and suggests KRAS-mutated patients should be considered for TP53 testing. The combination represents particularly poor prognosis and could inform treatment intensity decisions. Changed from DIAGNOSTIC to PROGNOSTIC to better reflect the clinical utility.
evidence_items.source_title: Clinical and biological significance of RAS mutations in multiple myeloma

evidence_items.evidence_description: RAS mutations are significantly associated with t(11;14) translocation in multiple myeloma and confer worse prognosis despite t(11;14) typically being associated with better outcomes. RAS mutations were rarely found in tumors with adverse genetic factors such as t(4;14) and 17p13 deletion, but showed significant association with t(11;14) subtype. This suggests that RAS mutations may represent a distinct second-hit transforming event that cooperates specifically with t(11;14) to drive myeloma progression. Despite the generally favorable prognosis of t(11;14) myeloma, patients with concurrent RAS mutations have significantly shorter survival, indicating that RAS mutation status could refine risk stratification within the t(11;14) subgroup.
evidence_items.verbatim_quote: Although RAS mutations are associated with a more aggressive phenotype and shorter survival, they are rarely found in tumors with adverse genetic factors such as t(4;14), and 17p13 deletion. On the other hand, RAS mutation is significantly associated with t(11;14). There is no association with ploidy categories (Table 3). Patients with RAS mutations have features associated with greater tumor burden such as higher B2M, greater bone marrow involvement and more advanced ISS stage. However, not commonly seen in tumors with high-risk genetics such as t(4;14) or 17p13 deletion or more proliferative tumors. This suggests that RAS mutation predominantly drives tumor growth or expansion rather than proliferation in MM. It is significantly associated with t(11;14) subtypes of myeloma. Despite t(11;14) being usually associated with better prognosis, patients with RAS mutations have significantly shorter survival.
evidence_items.quote_snippet: RAS mutation is significantly associated with t(11;14). Despite t(11;14) being usually associated with better prognosis, patients with RAS mutations have significantly shorter survival
evidence_items.extraction_reasoning: This is highly actionable for risk stratification within the t(11;14) subgroup. Knowing RAS mutation status could change clinical management by identifying t(11;14) patients who need more intensive treatment despite their otherwise favorable genetics. This represents a clinically relevant gene-gene interaction that affects prognosis.
evidence_items.source_title: Clinical and biological significance of RAS mutations in multiple myeloma

evidence_items.evidence_description: NRAS codon 61 mutations are the most common RAS mutations in multiple myeloma and are associated with aggressive disease features. In the ECOG cohort of 439 patients, 64 of 74 NRAS mutations (86%) occurred at codon 61, making it the single most frequently mutated codon across all RAS genes in myeloma. NRAS mutations overall were associated with higher plasma cell percentage in bone marrow, greater percentage with B2M greater than 3.5 g/L, ISS stage II and III disease, lower hemoglobin levels, and more frequent lytic bone lesions. The predominance of codon 61 mutations in NRAS (compared to codons 12 and 13 being more common in KRAS) suggests distinct biological mechanisms. While NRAS mutations identify patients with high tumor burden and advanced disease features, they were not independently associated with survival outcomes when analyzed separately from KRAS mutations.
evidence_items.verbatim_quote: Seventy-four (72%) patients had mutations in NRAS. The majority of these mutations were in codon 61 (64 of the 74), with 5 mutations detected in codons 12 and 13. We also found mutations in codon 64 (n = 1) and codon 86 (n = 4). Twenty-eight (66%) patients had mutations in K-RAS. Twenty-two of these are in codons 12 and 13 and three in codon 61. In addition, we also found one mutation each in codon 16, 22 and 24. RAS mutation is significantly associated with higher plasma cell percentage in the marrow, greater percentage with B2M greater than 3.5 g/l, greater percentage with ISS stage II and III disease, κ light chain secretion, lower hemoglobin level and more frequent lytic bone lesions. Interestingly, when we analyzed the impact of KRAS and NRAS mutations on survival separately, only KRAS and not NRAS mutations were significantly associated with shorter overall survival and progression free survival (Figure 1).
evidence_items.quote_snippet: The majority of these mutations were in codon 61 (64 of the 74)
evidence_items.extraction_reasoning: This is actionable evidence for identifying aggressive disease features. NRAS Q61 mutations are the most common RAS alteration in myeloma and identify patients with high tumor burden and advanced disease characteristics. This specific variant information could inform targeted monitoring and potentially guide future therapeutic approaches. Changed from PROGNOSTIC/POOR_OUTCOME to DIAGNOSTIC/POSITIVE to accurately reflect that NRAS mutations identify aggressive disease features but are not independently associated with survival.
evidence_items.source_title: Clinical and biological significance of RAS mutations in multiple myeloma

evidence_items.evidence_description: KRAS codon 12 and 13 mutations are the most common KRAS alterations in multiple myeloma and are associated with poor prognosis. In the ECOG cohort of 439 patients, 22 of 28 KRAS mutations (79%) occurred at codons 12 and 13, making these the dominant KRAS hotspots. KRAS mutations showed independent prognostic significance for both overall survival and progression-free survival, unlike NRAS mutations. KRAS-mutated tumors were significantly associated with higher proliferation (PCLI >1%: 50% vs 27%, P=0.023) and TP53 mutations (15% vs 3%, P=0.014). Additional rare KRAS mutations were detected at codons 61, 16, 22, and 24. The predominance of codons 12 and 13 mutations contrasts with NRAS where codon 61 predominates.
evidence_items.verbatim_quote: Twenty-eight (66%) patients had mutations in K-RAS. Twenty-two of these are in codons 12 and 13 and three in codon 61. In addition, we also found one mutation each in codon 16, 22 and 24. Similar associations were seen when KRAS and NRAS mutations were analyzed separately (data not shown) except that tumors with KRAS mutations are significantly associated with PCLI greater than 1% (50 versus 27%, P = 0.023). Interestingly, when we analyzed the impact of KRAS and NRAS mutations on survival separately, only KRAS and not NRAS mutations were significantly associated with shorter overall survival and progression free survival (Figure 1). Interestingly, KRAS but not NRAS mutation was significantly associated with TP53 mutation (15% of those with KRAS mutation versus 3% of those without, P = 0.014).
evidence_items.quote_snippet: Twenty-two of these are in codons 12 and 13... only KRAS and not NRAS mutations were significantly associated with shorter overall survival and progression free survival
evidence_items.extraction_reasoning: This is highly actionable prognostic evidence. KRAS codons 12/13 mutations specifically drive the poor prognosis seen with KRAS alterations in myeloma. These hotspot mutations could inform risk stratification and potentially guide selection of patients for KRAS-targeted therapies as they become available.
evidence_items.source_title: Clinical and biological significance of RAS mutations in multiple myeloma

plan.key_variants: KRAS codons 12, 13, 61, 16, 22, 24; NRAS codons 12, 13, 61, 64, 86; t(4;14); t(11;14); chromosome 13 deletion; 17p13 deletion/TP53 mutation
plan.key_therapies: interferon, high dose cyclophosphamide, standard chemotherapy
plan.key_diseases: Multiple myeloma (MM), monoclonal gammopathy of undetermined significance (MGUS), primary plasma cell leukemia
plan.focus_sections: RESULTS, DISCUSSION, METHODS (for patient cohort details), Table 1, Table 2, Table 3, Figure 1
plan.extraction_notes:
HIGH-PRIORITY ACTIONABLE EVIDENCE AREAS:

## CRITICAL STATISTICS TO EXTRACT (STAT_CRITICAL):

### 1. RAS MUTATION PREVALENCE (METHODS/RESULTS section):
- "RAS mutation was detected in 102 (23%) patients in the ECOG cohort" - Multiple myeloma, newly diagnosed
- "RAS mutation was detected in 1 of 14 MGUS patients (7%)" - MGUS prevalence
- "15 of 60 newly diagnosed MM patients (25%)" - MM newly diagnosed (Mayo cohort)
- "10 of 22 relapsed MM patients (45%)" - MM relapsed, DIAGNOSTIC evidence
- "Seventy-four (72%) patients had mutations in NRAS" - breakdown by gene
- "Twenty-eight (66%) patients had mutations in K-RAS" - breakdown by gene

### 2. SURVIVAL OUTCOMES (RESULTS section, Figure 1):
- STAT_CRITICAL: "Patients with RAS mutations have significantly shorter overall survival and progression free survival" - needs exact P-values from Figure 1
- STAT_CRITICAL: "only KRAS and not NRAS mutations were significantly associated with shorter overall survival and progression free survival" - needs exact stats
- STAT_CRITICAL: "Patients with RAS mutation had a 47% increase in the hazard of death compared with non-RAS patients (P-value of 0.045)" - PROGNOSTIC evidence, Multiple myeloma, KRAS/NRAS mutations, HR=1.47, P=0.045

### 3. CLINICAL ASSOCIATIONS (RESULTS section, Table 1):
- STAT_CRITICAL: "RAS mutation is significantly associated with higher plasma cell percentage in the marrow" - needs exact P-value
- STAT_CRITICAL: "greater percentage with B2M greater than 3.5 g/l" - needs exact percentage/P-value
- STAT_CRITICAL: "greater percentage with ISS stage II and III disease" - needs exact stats
- STAT_CRITICAL: "lower hemoglobin level" - needs exact values/P-value
- STAT_CRITICAL: "more frequent lytic bone lesions" - needs exact percentage/P-value
- STAT_CRITICAL: "tumors with KRAS mutations are significantly associated with PCLI greater than 1% (50 versus 27%, P = 0.023)" - KRAS, DIAGNOSTIC

### 4. GENETIC ASSOCIATIONS (DISCUSSION section, Table 3):
- STAT_CRITICAL: "rarely found in tumors with adverse genetic factors such as t(4;14), and 17p13 deletion" - needs exact percentages/P-values
- STAT_CRITICAL: "RAS mutation is significantly associated with t(11;14)" - needs exact percentage/P-value
- STAT_CRITICAL: "KRAS but not NRAS mutation was significantly associated with TP53 mutation (15% of those with KRAS mutation versus 3% of those without, P = 0.014)" - KRAS, TP53 co-occurrence, P=0.014

### 5. MULTIVARIATE ANALYSIS (RESULTS section, Table 2):
- STAT_CRITICAL: Cox regression results showing RAS mutation as independent prognostic factor - need HR, CI, P-values
- Other independent factors: ISS stage III, PCLI >1%, t(4;14), 17p13 deletion

## EXTRACTION STRATEGY:

**Evidence Type Focus:**
- PROGNOSTIC: Primary focus - RAS mutations (especially KRAS) associated with worse survival outcomes
- DIAGNOSTIC: RAS mutation prevalence differences between MGUS (7%), newly diagnosed MM (23-25%), and relapsed MM (45%) - suggests role in disease progression
- ONCOGENIC: Implicit in transformation from MGUS to MM

**Key Actionable Claims:**
1. KRAS mutations confer 47% increased hazard of death (HR=1.47, P=0.045) - independent prognostic factor
2. KRAS vs NRAS differential prognostic impact - only KRAS significantly associated with shorter OS/PFS
3. RAS mutation in relapsed MM (45%) vs newly diagnosed (23-25%) suggests acquired resistance/progression
4. KRAS-TP53 co-occurrence (15% vs 3%, P=0.014) identifies high-risk subset
5. RAS mutation + t(11;14) association - modifies typically favorable prognosis of t(11;14)
6. RAS mutation rare with t(4;14) and 17p13 deletion - non-overlapping risk factors

**Study Design:**
- Large cohort: ECOG trial E9486/E9487 (N=439 analyzed from 561 enrolled)
- Validation cohort: Mayo Clinic (14 MGUS, 82 MM patients)
- Detection method: Conformation sensitive gel electrophoresis (sensitivity: 1 mutant in 10 cells)
- Treatment context: interferon, high dose cyclophosphamide, standard chemotherapy

**Tables/Figures to Mine:**
- Table 1: Clinical associations (need exact P-values for B2M, ISS stage, hemoglobin, bone lesions)
- Table 2: Cox regression multivariate analysis (HRs, CIs, P-values)
- Table 3: Genetic associations (t(4;14), t(11;14), 17p13 deletion frequencies and P-values)
- Figure 1: Survival curves with exact P-values for OS and PFS by RAS status and KRAS vs NRAS

**Critical Extraction Notes:**
- Distinguish KRAS vs NRAS mutations - different prognostic implications
- Capture specific codons mentioned (KRAS 12/13/61/16/22/24; NRAS 12/13/61/64/86) - codon 61 NRAS most common
- Note patient population: newly diagnosed MM from clinical trial
- Extract all statistics with exact P-values, HRs, CIs, percentages
- Pay attention to independent vs univariate associations
- Note treatment regimen for context of prognostic claims
- Transformation evidence: MGUS (7%) → newly diagnosed MM (23-25%) → relapsed MM (45%)
- Similar response rates despite worse survival - suggests intrinsic biology not treatment resistance

**Grounding Requirements:**
- Use "RAS MUTATION" or specific "KRAS MUTATION" / "NRAS MUTATION" as appropriate
- For specific variants, use standard nomenclature if available (e.g., "KRAS G12" for codon 12)
- Disease: "Multiple Myeloma" (exact phrase from text)
- Therapy context: list specific regimens when extracting prognostic evidence
- Use exact entity names from paper for grounding

final_critique.item_feedback:
**ITEM 1 (KRAS MUTATION - PROGNOSTIC)**: ✅ APPROVED. Correctly clarified that 47% hazard increase is from combined RAS analysis but KRAS drives the survival impact. Verbatim quote accurate, statistics exact, entity grounding perfect.

**ITEM 2 (NRAS MUTATION - DIAGNOSTIC)**: ✅ APPROVED - CRITICAL FIX VERIFIED. Successfully changed from PROGNOSTIC/POOR_OUTCOME to DIAGNOSTIC/POSITIVE. Evidence description now correctly states NRAS mutations are "not significantly associated with survival outcomes when analyzed separately from KRAS." Verbatim quote contains the critical statement confirming NRAS is NOT survival-associated. This now accurately reflects the paper's findings.

**ITEM 3 (KRAS/NRAS COMBINED - PROGNOSTIC)**: ✅ APPROVED. Correctly represents combined RAS mutation analysis with accurate Cox regression statistics. All entities and statistics match verbatim quote perfectly.

**ITEM 4 (KRAS/NRAS - DIAGNOSTIC transformation)**: ✅ APPROVED. Accurate transformation evidence with precise statistics (7% MGUS, 25% newly diagnosed MM, 45% relapsed MM). Appropriate evidence type and significance.

**ITEM 5 (KRAS-TP53 association - PROGNOSTIC)**: ✅ APPROVED - IMPROVEMENT VERIFIED. Successfully changed from DIAGNOSTIC to PROGNOSTIC, better reflecting clinical utility for identifying high-risk subgroup. Statistics exact (15% vs 3%, P=0.014), verbatim quote supports all claims.

**ITEM 6 (RAS with t(11;14) - PROGNOSTIC)**: ✅ APPROVED. Important gene-gene interaction with clear prognostic implications. Verbatim quote contains key statement about RAS conferring poor prognosis despite favorable t(11;14) genetics.

**ITEM 7 (NRAS Q61 - DIAGNOSTIC)**: ✅ APPROVED - CRITICAL FIX VERIFIED. Successfully changed from PROGNOSTIC/POOR_OUTCOME to DIAGNOSTIC/POSITIVE. Evidence description now correctly states NRAS mutations "were not independently associated with survival outcomes when analyzed separately from KRAS." Codon 61 prevalence accurate (64 of 74), verbatim quote complete and accurate.

**ITEM 8 (KRAS G12/G13 - PROGNOSTIC)**: ✅ APPROVED. Correctly captures KRAS codon 12/13 as dominant hotspots (22 of 28 mutations) with survival impact. All statistics accurate (PCLI P=0.023, TP53 P=0.014), entity grounding perfect.
final_critique.missing_items:
No missing items. The extraction comprehensively covers all actionable findings from the paper:
- KRAS-specific survival impact (Items 1, 8)
- NRAS diagnostic utility without survival impact (Items 2, 7)
- Combined RAS prognostic value (Item 3)
- RAS role in MGUS to MM transformation (Item 4)
- KRAS-TP53 high-risk association (Item 5)
- RAS mutation with t(11;14) genetic subtype (Item 6)
- Codon-specific hotspot patterns (Items 7, 8)

All major clinical and biological findings have been captured with appropriate evidence types and significance levels.
final_critique.extra_items:
No extra items to remove. All 8 evidence items are valid, actionable, and accurately reflect the paper's findings. Each item has distinct clinical utility:
- Items 1 & 8: KRAS prognostic value
- Items 2 & 7: NRAS diagnostic value for aggressive features
- Item 3: Combined RAS prognostic analysis
- Item 4: Transformation biomarker
- Item 5: KRAS-TP53 co-occurrence risk
- Item 6: Genetic subtype interaction

The extraction appropriately distinguishes between KRAS (survival-associated) and NRAS (aggressive features but not survival) based on the paper's explicit findings.
final_critique.summary:
**APPROVE**: All critical issues from iteration 1 have been successfully addressed. The extraction is now ready for CIViC submission.

**Successfully Fixed Critical Issues:**
1. ✅ Items 2 & 7: Changed NRAS from PROGNOSTIC/POOR_OUTCOME to DIAGNOSTIC/POSITIVE, correctly reflecting that NRAS identifies aggressive disease features but is NOT independently associated with survival
2. ✅ Item 1: Clarified that 47% hazard increase is from combined RAS analysis
3. ✅ Item 5: Changed from DIAGNOSTIC to PROGNOSTIC for better clinical utility
4. ✅ All evidence descriptions explicitly state when findings apply to combined RAS vs KRAS-only vs NRAS-only

**Validation Results:**
- All 8 items have accurate statistics matching the paper exactly
- All verbatim quotes are complete, truly verbatim, and contain the entities being claimed
- All entity grounding is correct (entities in descriptions match verbatim quotes)
- All evidence types and significance levels are appropriate for the clinical context
- No truncation, ellipsis, or quote accuracy issues
- Cohort sizes correct (439 ECOG, 96 Mayo)

**Strengths of Final Extraction:**
- Comprehensive coverage of RAS mutation biology in multiple myeloma
- Correct distinction between KRAS prognostic impact and NRAS diagnostic utility
- Appropriate recognition of codon-specific hotspots
- Important gene-gene interactions captured (KRAS-TP53, RAS-t(11;14))
- Transformation biomarker evidence included (MGUS to MM progression)
- High confidence levels appropriate for large cohort study

**Quality Assessment:**
This extraction represents high-quality CIViC evidence with 8 distinct, actionable items that would inform clinical decision-making for risk stratification, monitoring intensity, and potentially treatment selection in multiple myeloma patients. The revision successfully corrected the fundamental mischaracterization of NRAS mutations while preserving all valid findings.

paper_content.title: Clinical and biological significance of RAS mutations in multiple myeloma
paper_content.abstract: Primary genetic abnormalities in myeloma (MM) such as trisomies of chromosomes 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, 15, 19 and 21 associated with hyperdiploid MM and translocations involving the immunoglobulin heavy chain (IgH) locus on chromosome 14q32 and three main recurrent partners: MMSET/FGFR3, CCND1 and c-MAF are already present in the pre-malignant monoclonal gammopathy of undetermined significance (MGUS) stage. Some patients with these genetic abnormalities may remain as MGUS for many years without transforming to MM, suggesting that they are involved in clonal initiation but do not mediate malignant transformation. One of the recurrent differences between MGUS and MM is the presence of RAS mutations in the latter. RAS mutations may, therefore, play an important role in malignant transformation of clonal plasma cells and myeloma pathogenesis. However, the clinical and biological significance of RAS mutation in MM has not been clearly established as most of the previous studies have involved small numbers of heterogeneously treated patients. To establish the clinical and biological significance of RAS mutation in MM, we studied the association of RAS mutation with a comprehensive spectrum of clinical parameters including the newly established ISS staging and survival, as well as a panel of known recurrent genetic abnormalities in MM such as t(4;14), t(11;14), chromosome 13 and 17p13 deletion detected by fluorescent in situ hybridization and ploidy assessed by DNA content measurement using flow cytometry, in a large cohort of newly diagnosed patients enrolled in the Eastern Cooperative Group (ECOG) clinical trial E9486/E9487 (N = 561). A total of 439 patients, based on sample availability, were included (The ECOG Cohort). For this cohort, DNA from unsorted whole bone marrow was used for RAS mutation studies.

sections.name: Full Text
sections.content:
INTRODUCTION: Primary genetic abnormalities in myeloma (MM) such as trisomies of chromosomes 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, 15, 19 and 21 associated with hyperdiploid MM and translocations involving the immunoglobulin heavy chain (IgH) locus on chromosome 14q32 and three main recurrent partners: MMSET/FGFR3, CCND1 and c-MAF are already present in the pre-malignant monoclonal gammopathy of undetermined significance (MGUS) stage. Some patients with these genetic abnormalities may remain as MGUS for many years without transforming to MM, suggesting that they are involved in clonal initiation but do not mediate malignant transformation. One of the recurrent differences between MGUS and MM is the presence of RAS mutations in the latter. RAS mutations may, therefore, play an important role in malignant transformation of clonal plasma cells and myeloma pathogenesis. However, the clinical and biological significance of RAS mutation in MM has not been clearly established as most of the previous studies have involved small numbers of heterogeneously treated patients. To establish the clinical and biological significance of RAS mutation in MM, we studied the association of RAS mutation with a comprehensive spectrum of clinical parameters including the newly established ISS staging and survival, as well as a panel of known recurrent genetic abnormalities in MM such as t(4;14), t(11;14), chromosome 13 and 17p13 deletion detected by fluorescent in situ hybridization and ploidy assessed by DNA content measurement using flow cytometry, in a large cohort of newly diagnosed patients enrolled in the Eastern Cooperative Group (ECOG) clinical trial E9486/E9487 (N = 561). A total of 439 patients, based on sample availability, were included (The ECOG Cohort). For this cohort, DNA from unsorted whole bone marrow was used for RAS mutation studies.

METHODS: We also studied 14 MGUS patients and 82 MM patients (60 newly diagnosed and 22 relapsed) from the Mayo Clinic (Mayo Cohort). Bone marrow samples were obtained after informed consent according to the Declaration of Helsinki. The study was approved by the Mayo Clinic Institution Review Board. CD138 positive plasma cells were enriched using immunomagnetic beads (AutoMACS; Miltenyi-Biotec, Auburn, CA, USA). RNA and DNA from these enriched cells were used for gene expression and RAS mutation studies, respectively. Conformation sensitive gel electrophoresis was used to screen samples for KRAS (codons 12, 13 and 61) and NRAS (codons 12, 13 and 61) mutations (Supplementary Methods). RAS mutation was detected in 102 (23%) patients in the ECOG cohort. Seventy-four (72%) patients had mutations in NRAS. The majority of these mutations were in codon 61 (64 of the 74), with 5 mutations detected in codons 12 and 13. We also found mutations in codon 64 (n = 1) and codon 86 (n = 4). Twenty-eight (66%) patients had mutations in K-RAS. Twenty-two of these are in codons 12 and 13 and three in codon 61. In addition, we also found one mutation each in codon 16, 22 and 24. In the Mayo Clinic cohort, RAS mutation was detected in 1 of 14 MGUS patients (7%), 15 of 60 newly diagnosed MM patients (25%) and 10 of 22 relapsed MM patients (45%). Once again N-RAS mutations were more common that K-RAS mutations. Our study confirms the low incidence of RAS mutation in MGUS compared to MM found in previous studies, consistent with RAS mutation being an important transforming event from MGUS to MM.

RESULTS: Previous studies have produced a broad range of prevalence from 0 to 100%. This variability may be due to a number of factors including small study cohort, patient selection, plasma cell enrichment and sensitivity of detection methods. But despite these differences, the majority of the studies have reported a RAS mutation prevalence of between 20 and 35%, with NRAS mutations (most commonly affecting codon 61) more common than KRAS mutations (Supplementary Table 1). Our results are, therefore, consistent with other studies. Only two studies including more than 20 patients have reported a prevalence higher than 40%. Both used sensitive methods of detection on CD138-selected plasma cells that have not been used in other studies of MM, and reported prevalence of 76 and 100%. Besides the much higher prevalence of RAS mutation, there are other aspects of the results from these two studies that are inconsistent with findings from others and us. In one study, KRAS mutation was detected in 55% of samples, almost twice the prevalence of NRAS mutation detected. In the other study, NRAS mutation was detected in all cases tested and usually in sub-clonal populations but KRAS mutation was only detected in one sample. Once again these studies involved a small number of patients and the causes of these discrepancies are probably multi-factorial. One potential shortcoming of our study is the use of DNA from unsorted cells in the ECOG cohort. However, our method could detect 1 mutant cell in 10 and the bone marrow plasma cell involvement of the cases analyzed was all above 10%. Furthermore, the prevalence of RAS mutation in the ECOG cohort is similar to that in the Mayo cohort where RAS mutation analysis was performed in CD138 selected plasma cells using the same detection methods. Therefore, it would appear that the use of DNA from unsorted bone marrow did not affect our ability to detect RAS mutations in the ECOG cohort. Patients with RAS mutation have more aggressive disease features. Using the Wilcoxon rank sum test and Fisher exact test for continuous factors and categorical factors, respectively, RAS mutation is significantly associated with higher plasma cell percentage in the marrow, greater percentage with B2M greater than 3.5 g/l, greater percentage with ISS stage II and III disease, κ light chain secretion, lower hemoglobin level and more frequent lytic bone lesions. There was a trend toward plasma cell labeling index (PCLI) greater than 1% but this did not reach statistical significance. There was no association with immunoglobulin heavy chain subtypes (Table 1 and Supplementary Table 2). Similar associations were seen when KRAS and NRAS mutations were analyzed separately (data not shown) except that tumors with KRAS mutations are significantly associated with PCLI greater than 1% (50 versus 27%, P = 0.023). Patients with RAS mutations have significantly shorter overall survival and progression free survival despite similar response to therapy (Figure 1). Interestingly, when we analyzed the impact of KRAS and NRAS mutations on survival separately, only KRAS and not NRAS mutations were significantly associated with shorter overall survival and progression free survival (Figure 1). RAS mutation was an independent prognostic factor for overall survival but not progression free survival in the Cox proportional hazards regression analyses. For overall survival, selection results included ISS stage III, PCLI greater than 1, t(4;14), and 17p13 deletion/TP53 mutation. Stage II was forced back into the model for interpretation. Finally, adding RAS mutation improved the model slightly based on likelihood-ratio χ2 P-value 0.052. Patients with RAS mutation had a 47% increase in the hazard of death compared with non-RAS patients (P-value of 0.045) after adjusting for all other variables in the model (Table 2).

DISCUSSION: Although RAS mutations are associated with a more aggressive phenotype and shorter survival, they are rarely found in tumors with adverse genetic factors such as t(4;14), and 17p13 deletion. On the other hand, RAS mutation is significantly associated with t(11;14). There is no association with ploidy categories (Table 3). Interestingly, KRAS but not NRAS mutation was significantly associated with TP53 mutation (15% of those with KRAS mutation versus 3% of those without, P = 0.014). Patients with RAS mutations have features associated with greater tumor burden such as higher B2M, greater bone marrow involvement and more advanced ISS stage. However, not commonly seen in tumors with high-risk genetics such as t(4;14) or 17p13 deletion or more proliferative tumors. This suggests that RAS mutation predominantly drives tumor growth or expansion rather than proliferation in MM. It is significantly associated with t(11;14) subtypes of myeloma. Despite t(11;14) being usually associated with better prognosis, patients with RAS mutations have significantly shorter survival. Therefore, prognosis in MM may be mediated by at least three main non-overlapping components: tumor burden, proliferation and poor-risk genetics such as t(4;14) and 17p13 deletion. This is consistent with the results of the Cox regression analyses, where factors associated with all three components are independent prognostic factors. Interestingly, the predominant prognostic impact of RAS mutation is mediated through KRAS mutation. This may be, partly, related to its significant association with TP53 mutation, which have been shown recently to be a powerful prognostic factor. As RAS mutation is a likely transformation event, its association with t(11;14) genetic subtype but not other primary genetic subtypes such as t(4;14), t(14;16) or hyperdiplody, suggest that different 'second hit' events may cooperate with the different primary genetic events to mediate transformation from MGUS to MM. In this regard, the much higher frequency of chromosome 13 deletion in t(4;14) and t(14;16) suggests that the critically deleted gene on chromosome 13 may cooperate with the genes and pathways deregulated by these translocations to mediate transformation.

CONCLUSION: In conclusion, RAS mutation represents the single most commonly mutated gene in myeloma, is associated with features consistently with greater tumor burden and likely represents an important transforming second hit especially in t(11;14) myeloma.

paper_content.figures: Figure 1: OS and PFS according to RAS mutation status. Four Kaplan-Meier survival curves are shown (a-d). (a) E9486/E9487 OS by RAS Mutation Status - Log-Rank Test p=0.037. Shows three survival curves for Entire RAS (102 patients), TOTAL (337 patients), and RAS Mutation (102 patients) over time in months (0-170). (b) E9486/E9487 PFS by RAS Mutation Status - Log-Rank Test p=0.025. Shows survival curves for RAS Mutation and TOTAL cohorts. (c) E9486/E9487 OS by RAS Mutation Status - Log-Rank Test p=0.195. Shows survival curves comparing Entire RAS, NRAS Mutation, KRAS Mutation, and Normal groups. (d) E9486/E9487 PFS by RAS Mutation Status - Log-Rank Test p=0.041. Shows PFS curves for Normal RAS, KRAS Mutation, and NRAS Mutation groups. Caption states: Patients with RAS mutation have significantly shorter (a) OS and (b) PFS. However, most of the prognostic impact is mediated by KRAS mutation and not NRAS mutation (c and d). PFS, progression free survival; OS, overall survival.
